votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the string splitting logic and the precondition and specification exactly match the Coq definitions.
The Python code correctly implements the string splitting logic, character filtering, and precondition checks exactly as specified in the Coq code.

The Python code faithfully implements the logic of the Coq specification. The iterative `words_string_aux` in Python correctly mirrors the recursive accumulation and splitting logic of the Coq function, handling delimiters, word accumulation, and edge cases (like consecutive delimiters or empty input) in the same way.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The is_delimiter function checks for comma and space. The words_string_aux function iteratively builds words by accumulating characters until a delimiter is found, matching the recursive Coq version. The precondition checks that all characters are letters (A-Z, a-z), comma, or space. The spec checks that output equals words_string(s). Both produce the same concrete results for all valid inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The is_delimiter function checks for comma and space. The words_string_aux function iteratively builds words by accumulating characters until a delimiter is found, matching the recursive Coq definition. The precondition checks that all characters are letters (A-Z, a-z), comma, or space. The spec checks that output equals words_string(s). All concrete behaviors match.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. The is_delimiter function checks for comma or space. The words_string_aux function iteratively builds words by accumulating characters until a delimiter is found, then appending the accumulated word to results - this matches the Coq recursive definition. The precondition checks that all characters are letters (A-Z, a-z), comma, or space. The spec checks that output equals the result of words_string. Both implementations will produce the same concrete results for any valid input string.

Both implementations split the input on commas and spaces, ignore consecutive/leading/trailing delimiters, and return the same list of words; the precondition and spec checks also match the Coq definitions.

Python mirrors Coq’s delimiter check (comma or space) and word accumulation, including handling of consecutive/edge delimiters. The precondition and spec are also implemented equivalently, and the final join matches Coq’s string_of_list_ascii.

Python mirrors Coq: same delimiter test (comma/space), same accumulation and skipping of consecutive delimiters, no empty words, and identical final joining. The precondition (letters, comma, space) matches via ord checks. Spec is equality to words_string, which both implement identically.
